In this scenario, http:\\ is ignored and only the a\b:c\d@foo.com is matched. `a` becomes the hostname and the rest of the URL is the pathname. It is only slightly inconsistent with the WHATWG spec in the hostname resolution. The actual transforming backslashes to slashes is also done in the WHATWG spec.

description: Trade-off: Do not match the trailing '..' as a pathname segment. Treat it as a part of the surrounding text.
Match the non-trailing '..' as a pathname segment.
Trade-off: Do not match the trailing '..' as a pathname segment. Treat it as a part of the surrounding text. This means the final pathname contains 3 forward slashes.
Trade-off: Do not match the trailing '..' as a pathname segment. Treat it as a part of the surrounding text. This means the final pathname ends with 2 forward slashes.
Trade-off: Truncate the matched URL at the first whitespace character.
Trade-off: Do not match the trailing parenthesis as a pathname segment. Treat it as a part of the surrounding text – it's more likely to be that.
Trade-off: Truncate the matched URL at the first whitespace character.
Trade-off: Do not match initial \ as a part of the pathname if it's not preceded by a forward slash. It is unlikely that anything starting with \ is the intended pathname.
